input.background: Acute lower respiratory tract infection is a leading cause of death among infants and under-5s in developing countries. A simple test is needed to differentiate between infants with acute respiratory infection who have lower respiratory tract infection (LRI) and require antibiotics, and those with upper respiratory tract infection (URI). The study explores the usefulness of respiratory rate as a diagnostic tool for this purpose.
input.data:
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Respiratory rate (breaths/min) & Number of children (\%) \\
\hline & LRI & URI \\
\hline 0-30 & 1 (1\%) & 16 (11\%) \\
\hline 31-40 & 4 (3\%) & 77 (51\%) \\
\hline 41-50 & 10 (7\%) & 46 (30\%) \\
\hline 51-60 & 41 (29\%) & 9 (6\%) \\
\hline 61+ & 86 (61\%) & 3 (2\%) \\
\hline Total & 142 (100\%) & 151 (100\%) \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
input.question: If general practitioners currently treat 80% of infants with respiratory tract infection (LRI or URI) with antibiotics, what would be the effect on antibiotic usage of the policy suggested in (c)?

output.answer:
If all infants above the cut-off of >50 breaths/min are treated, 89% of LRI infants and 8% of URI infants would be treated. Assuming a prevalence of LRI as 3%, the proportion treated would be:

0.89 × 0.03 + 0.08 × 0.97 = 0.10.

Thus, only 10% of infants would be treated with antibiotics, representing a significant cost saving. Using a cut-off of 40 breaths/min, the proportion treated would be:

0.96 × 0.03 + 0.38 × 0.97 = 0.40.

This would still cost half as much as the current policy.
